title: Somatic Therapy for Survivors of Poverty and Economic Trauma
excerpt: Explore how somatic therapy aids survivors of poverty and economic trauma in healing body-based stress responses and reclaiming safety.
body:
Understanding Economic Trauma and Its Somatic Impact

Economic trauma and poverty are often overlooked sources of chronic stress and trauma. Unlike a single traumatic event, poverty exerts a relentless pressure on the body’s nervous system through ongoing insecurity, scarcity, and threat. This kind of trauma is not just psychological but deeply somatic—rooted in the body’s lived experience of survival under threat.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, emphasizes that trauma is stored in the body and nervous system, not just the mind. Economic trauma triggers a persistent state of hypervigilance, dysregulation, and survival responses that shape physiology and behavior over time (van der Kolk, 2014). Survivors often report feeling trapped in fight/flight/freeze patterns, exhaustion, and a pervasive sense of unsafety, even when objective danger may lessen.

The Neuroscience Behind Poverty-Related Trauma

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a crucial framework for understanding how poverty and economic hardship hijack the nervous system. Chronic financial stress activates the sympathetic nervous system, increasing heart rate and stress hormones like cortisol, while also potentially triggering dorsal vagal shutdown—a freeze response characterized by numbness, dissociation, and withdrawal (Porges, 2011).

These physiological states are adaptive in the moment, helping individuals survive scarcity and threat. But when prolonged, they impair emotional regulation, decision-making, and physical health. Dan Siegel’s concept of window of tolerance explains how chronic economic stress narrows a survivor’s capacity to stay present and regulated, leading to overwhelm or shutdown (Siegel, 1999).

Why Traditional Talk Therapy Often Falls Short

Many survivors of poverty and economic trauma have tried talk therapy without lasting relief. This is because trauma is often encoded in the body’s implicit memory, outside of conscious narrative and verbal reasoning. As Peter Levine, founder of Somatic Experiencing, points out, trauma lives in sensation and autonomic nervous system dysregulation rather than in the story itself (Levine, 2010).

Talk therapy alone may inadvertently reinforce cognitive avoidance or retraumatization if it does not address the body’s somatic experience. Without integrating body-based methods, survivors can remain stuck in patterns of chronic stress, reactivity, and dissociation.

What Is Somatic Therapy and How Does It Help?

Somatic therapy is a body-centered therapeutic approach that focuses on tuning into bodily sensations, movements, and autonomic regulation to heal trauma. It helps survivors access, process, and release stored trauma held in the body through mindful awareness, breath work, movement, and relational safety.

Pat Ogden, a leading figure in somatic psychology, explains that trauma disrupts the natural flow of energy and movement in the body. Somatic therapy restores this flow by helping clients reconnect with bodily sensations and develop new patterns of self-regulation (Ogden, 2015).

Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal-informed therapy emphasizes creating a therapeutic environment that helps clients feel safe enough to engage their social engagement system—the ventral vagal complex—which is essential for healing and connection (Dana, 2018).

Key Somatic Techniques for Survivors of Economic Trauma

Survivors of poverty-related trauma can benefit from specific somatic tools that address both nervous system dysregulation and chronic survival patterns:

  • Tracking Sensations: Learning to notice subtle bodily sensations without judgment helps develop interoceptive awareness and creates a window for regulation.
  • Grounding and Centering: Techniques such as feeling feet on the floor or hands on the body help anchor survivors in the present moment and counter dissociation.
  • Breath Regulation: Deep, slow breathing stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system, reducing sympathetic arousal and promoting calm.
  • Pendulation: Moving attention between states of activation and relaxation helps renegotiate stuck trauma patterns (Levine, 2010).
  • Movement and Expression: Gentle stretching, shaking, or expressive movement can release tension and restore natural flow of energy.
  • Resourcing and Safe Place Visualization: Creating internal or external anchors of safety supports nervous system regulation and builds resilience.

Addressing the Socioeconomic Context in Therapy

Economic trauma is inseparable from systemic issues like classism, discrimination, and social injustice. Gabor Maté stresses the importance of recognizing how social determinants shape trauma and healing capacity (Maté, 2018). Therapists working with survivors must validate the reality of financial hardship and avoid pathologizing clients’ responses.

Integrating somatic therapy with awareness of socioeconomic context empowers clients to reclaim agency over their bodies and lives. It also supports building community and connection, essential antidotes to isolation and shame common in poverty.

Case Example: Anna’s Journey Through Somatic Healing

Anna, a survivor of generational poverty, struggled with chronic anxiety, fatigue, and feelings of invisibility despite years of talk therapy. Through somatic therapy, she learned to notice when her body tensed during financial stress and practiced grounding and breath exercises to calm her nervous system.

Gradually, Anna became aware of how her body held fear and hypervigilance, and with her therapist’s guidance, she practiced pendulation and gentle movement to release trapped energy. She also explored feelings of shame related to poverty and found new ways to create safety within herself and her relationships.

Anna’s case illustrates how somatic therapy addresses both the physiological and emotional dimensions of economic trauma, facilitating deeper healing beyond words.

How to Find a Somatic Therapist Experienced with Economic Trauma

Not all somatic therapists are equally familiar with poverty-related trauma and its unique challenges. When searching for a provider, look for those who:

  • Explicitly mention experience or training in working with socioeconomic trauma
  • Use trauma-informed, Polyvagal-informed, or Somatic Experiencing approaches
  • Demonstrate cultural humility and awareness of systemic oppression
  • Create a collaborative, client-led approach that centers safety and empowerment

Starting with a consultation can help you assess if the therapist’s style and expertise fit your needs.

Conclusion: Reclaiming Safety and Self Through the Body

Economic trauma embeds itself deeply in the nervous system and body, shaping how survivors experience safety, stress, and connection. Somatic therapy offers a powerful pathway to reclaim safety by addressing trauma where it lives—in the body’s sensations, movements, and autonomic rhythms.

By learning to regulate their nervous systems and release trapped survival energy, survivors of poverty can break free from chronic stress cycles and move toward resilience and wellbeing. As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, healing trauma requires a body-based approach that honors the lived experience beyond words (van der Kolk, 2014).

“Trauma is not just an event that took place long ago; it is also the imprint left by that experience on mind, brain, and body.” — Bessel van der Kolk

References

  • Bessel van der Kolk (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  • Peter Levine (2010). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Stephen Porges (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton & Company.
  • Deb Dana (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton & Company.
  • Pat Ogden (2015). Trauma and the Body. Norton & Company.
  • Gabor Maté (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. North Atlantic Books.
  • Dan Siegel (1999). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for economic trauma?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body's sensations and nervous system regulation rather than just verbal processing, addressing trauma stored physically. This helps survivors of economic trauma release chronic stress held in the body.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if I don’t have access to a professional therapist?
faqs.a: While professional guidance is ideal, many somatic techniques like grounding, breathwork, and sensation tracking can be practiced safely on your own. However, complex trauma benefits from support to navigate intense sensations.

faqs.q: Why is the nervous system important in healing poverty-related trauma?
faqs.a: The nervous system regulates stress responses and safety signals. Economic trauma keeps the nervous system in survival mode, impairing regulation. Healing involves restoring nervous system balance for emotional and physical wellbeing.

faqs.q: How do systemic issues like classism affect somatic therapy outcomes?
faqs.a: Systemic oppression compounds trauma and can create mistrust or shame. Therapists aware of these dynamics create safer spaces and tailor approaches, improving healing outcomes for survivors of poverty.
